mappings.rationaleAI: Classical mechanics and electromagnetism as fundamental physics topics connect to principles of classical mechanics (A), fundamental parameters of matter, space, motion, and time (A.1), and dynamics of motion systems (A.3). Relativistic mechanics connects to section C.

Historical development from Kepler's laws through Newtonian gravity (A.3.b.v) to Einstein's general relativity (C). Detection of gravitational waves and dark energy connect to celestial mechanics (B) and relativistic mechanics (C). The relationship between gravitational and inertial mass is directly addressed (C.3).

Special and general theories of relativity including time dilation, length contraction, and mass-energy equivalence (A.3.e.iii) directly address relativistic mechanics (C), mechanical foundations (C.1), relativistic kinematics (C.2), and the relationship between gravitational and inertial mass (C.3).

Fluid dynamics and hydrodynamics directly address fluid mechanics (F), including general properties of fluids (F.1), fluid statics and equilibrium (F.2), buoyancy (F.2.b), frictionless flow (F.3.a), laminar and turbulent flow (F.3.b), boundary layers and drag (F.3.c), and compressible flow with shock waves (F.3.d).
Galileo, Newton, and Kepler as transformative figures in physics connect to the historical development of dynamics and kinetics (A.3.b), including Newton's laws of motion and universal gravitation.

Newton's contributions to mechanics including the Principia directly address Newton's laws of motion (A.3.b.i), the law of universal gravitation (A.3.b.v), and Kepler's laws of planetary motion (A.3.b.vi). His work on celestial mechanics relates to section B.

Galileo's contributions to physics including his break from Aristotelian physics and the development of the laws of motion connect to kinetics (A.3.b), Newton's laws predecessors (A.3.b.i), motion under constant force (A.3.b.ii), and ballistics (A.3.b.iii).

Material properties including hardness and flexibility relate to stress dynamics of elastic materials (D) and elasticity (D.1). Study of material strength and fabrication connects to yield strength of materials, fracture and fatigue (G.3).

Viscosity and adhesion in polymers, colloids, and foams connect to general properties of fluids (F.1). Brownian motion linking molecular-scale thermal motion to macroscopic behavior relates to rheological phenomena (G) and continuum mechanics (G.1).

Mathematical modeling in physics and engineering traces from Euclid through Descartes, connecting to Lagrange's and Hamilton's equations of motion (A.6.d) as examples of applied mathematical methods in mechanics.

Sensitive dependence on initial conditions in deterministic systems, including pendulum dynamics, connects to simple harmonic motion (A.3.a.iv) and stiffness in mechanical vibrations (A.4.b). Nonlinear dynamics is fundamental to complex mechanical systems.
States of matter including solids, liquids, gases, and plasmas provide context for the fundamental parameters of classical mechanics (A.1) and general properties of fluids (F.1).

The nature of space and time in Newtonian physics and special relativity directly relates to fundamental parameters of classical mechanics (A.1) and the mechanical foundations of special relativity (C.1).

Mechanical waves in solids and wave properties including reflection and diffraction connect to vibrations of elastic bodies (E), natural and forced vibrations (E.1), and types of vibrational waves (E.3).

Sound wave behavior including frequency, pitch, and resonance connects to vibrations of elastic bodies (E), natural vibrations (E.1), vibrators and energy sources (E.2), and behavior of materials undergoing vibration (E.4).

Beams, bridges, and structural analysis connect to statics and equilibrium (A.2), stress dynamics of elastic materials (D), stress-strain relationships (D.1), elasticity in crystalline bodies (D.2), and yield strength, fracture, and fatigue (G.3).
Engineering harnessing natural phenomena relates broadly to principles of classical mechanics (A), stress dynamics (D), and fluid mechanics (F) as foundational engineering disciplines.

Planetary system structure, galactic dynamics, and angular momentum in celestial phenomena directly address celestial mechanics (B), its scope and history (B.1), and the two-body problem with orbital perturbations (B.2).
Planetary orbital dynamics and solar system mechanics connect to celestial mechanics (B) and the two-body problem with perturbations affecting planetary orbits (B.2).

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationaleAI: Gravitational interactions of the Moon and Sun with ocean water connect to Newton's law of universal gravitation (A.3.b.v). Tidal dynamics in oceans relate to fluid statics (F.2) and buoyancy and stability of floating bodies (F.2.b).

Calculus, differentiation, and integration applied to fluid dynamics and optimization connect to Lagrange's and Hamilton's equations (A.6.d) and general two- and three-dimensional flow mathematical conditions (F.3.c).
Kepler, Newton, and gravitational theory in astronomical history directly connect to the scope and history of celestial mechanics (B.1).
Orbital dynamics and planetary formation processes connect to celestial mechanics (B) and the two-body problem with perturbations (B.2).
